note: Single source of truth for Oara / OmniRace. Every entry below is a REAL, verified source confirmed against PubMed/PMC and approved by Adam (2026-06-04). The analysis cites ONLY entries here — no entry, no claim (CLAUDE.md, First Law). Do not add fabricated or AI-recalled entries; follow evidence/README.md.

papers.title: An analysis of the pacing strategy adopted by elite competitors in 2000 m rowing
papers.notes: Citation, DOI and segment percentages confirmed against PMC full text (PMC1725010). Sample: 948 on-water race profiles (of 1612) from the 2000 Olympic Games and 2001–2002 World Championships, plus the top 170 at the 2001–2002 British Indoor Rowing Championships.

papers.authors: Dimakopoulou E, Zacharogiannis E, Chairopoulou C, Kaloupsis S, Platanou T
papers.title: The effect of pacing strategy on physiological, kinetic and performance variables during simulated rowing ergometer
papers.source: Journal of Sports Medicine and Physical Fitness 58(7–8):1006–1013
papers.notes: Citation, DOI and conclusion confirmed via PubMed (PMID 28222575). Sample: 15 adolescent male rowers (mean ~15 y), 2 km ergometer, three conditions (self-selected vs even vs negative −4%). Small/young sample — treat as suggestive.

papers.authors: Gee TI, French DN, Gibbon KC, Thompson KG
papers.title: Consistency of pacing and metabolic responses during 2000-m rowing ergometry
papers.source: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance 8(1):70–76
papers.notes: Citation, DOI and finding confirmed via PubMed (PMID 22868257). Sample: 14 well-trained male rowers, three 2000 m ergometer trials. Mainly a habituation/methodology point.

claims.statement: Elite 2000 m crews row the first 500 m fastest — about 103% of their average race speed — then settle to roughly 98–99% through the middle with a slight lift in the final 500 m (a fast-start, mildly reverse-J profile). This held regardless of finishing position or sex.
claims.quote: rowers performed the first 500 m of the race faster than subsequent sectors
claims.locator: Results; on-water sector speeds 103.3%, 99.0%, 98.3%, 99.7% of average
claims.caveats: Describes what elite championship heavyweights DO, not what is optimal for a club crew; on-water, not ergometer. Use the percentages as a benchmark for the shape, not as a target time.

claims.statement: Mean elite on-water 2000 m pace profile by 500 m sector, expressed as % of average race speed.

data.basis: on-water 500 m sectors, % of average race speed

claims.caveats: Mean of elite championship heavyweight crews; an individual or club crew need not match it.

claims.statement: When the same rowers were made to row a 2 km erg at an imposed even pace or a negative (−4%) pace versus their own self-selected pace, the self-selected (parabolic, fast-start) profile produced the shortest time and a higher stroke rate for the same physiological cost.
claims.quote: SSP (parabolic-shaped profile) allowed rowers to cover the 2-km distance at higher stroke rate and in shorter performance time compared to negative and even pacing strategies presenting same physiological responses
claims.strength: experimental (within-subject, three imposed conditions) — but small, young sample
claims.caveats: n=15 adolescent (~15 y) male rowers; ergometer. Suggestive, not definitive; may not generalise to senior or on-water crews.

claims.statement: Well-trained rowers settled into a reverse-J pacing pattern with an end-spurt — increased power output over the final 500 m — once habituated to repeated 2 km erg tests; a positive (front-loaded) profile on the first trial shifted toward this with familiarisation.
claims.quote: trial 1 ... featured a higher starting power output and more progressive decrease in power, to trials 2 and 3, which were characterized by a more conservative start and an end spurt with increased power output during the final 500 m
claims.caveats: n=14 well-trained male rowers; ergometer. The headline practical point is that a habituation trial is needed before testing.
